sections.title: Executive Summary
sections.summary: Basis-segregated summary of measurable lanes and permanent limitations.

sections.title: Definitions and Methodology
sections.summary: Primary legal and accounting definitions that bind the rest of the publication.

sections.title: International Assistance
sections.summary: Aggregate foreign-assistance obligations and disbursements with unresolved capture share.

sections.title: Ukraine and Israel Examples
sections.summary: Illustrative, non-additive examples showing stage differences and beneficiary-chain complexity.

sections.title: Military Aid
sections.summary: Net-new military lanes separated from already-counted foreign assistance and non-taxpayer flows.

sections.title: Refugee Resettlement
sections.summary: Domestic refugee and entrant assistance with unresolved provider-capture and beneficiary-split limits.

sections.title: Medicaid / Emergency Medical
sections.summary: Emergency-medical assistance lane with an explicit federal-only share gap.

sections.title: Food Assistance
sections.summary: Eligibility structure and limits of SNAP attribution without unsupported noncitizen dollar estimates.

sections.title: Cash Welfare / Income
sections.summary: SSI and welfare lanes where direct-cash structure is more measurable than many domestic programs.

sections.title: Federal Housing
sections.summary: Housing eligibility and proration mechanics without a published status-specific outlay ledger.

sections.title: Education / Public Services
sections.summary: Legal and eligibility structure for K-12 and student-aid analysis where status spending is not cleanly published.

sections.title: State-Administered Federal Dollars
sections.summary: A reconciliation lens for routed federal dollars, not a net-new spending subtotal.

sections.title: Programs Without Citizenship Breakouts
sections.summary: Gap register for programs where the public record does not support a defensible status-based spending figure.

sections.title: Conservative Total
sections.summary: A reproducible set of basis-segregated subtotals rather than one blended grand total.

sections.title: What Is Missing
sections.summary: Publication-wide limitations register preserving unresolved evidence constraints.

sections.title: Final Argument
sections.summary: Closing synthesis bounded by the same evidence and basis-separation rules as the rest of Version 1.0.

claims.title: Executive summary keeps measurable lanes on their native bases
claims.summary: The opening section preserves lane separation instead of collapsing obligations, outlays, cumulative military lines, and domestic program examples into one number.

claims.title: Methodology distinguishes legal status categories and number types
claims.summary: Qualified status categories, waiting-period rules, appropriations, obligations, outlays, and transfer values are treated as distinct analytical objects.

claims.title: International assistance obligations and disbursements are not interchangeable
claims.summary: Section 3 preserves separate accounting states while carrying the unresolved beneficiary-capture share as an open question.

claims.title: Ukraine and Israel examples are illustrative and non-additive
claims.summary: Section 4 exists to show stage differences and beneficiary chains without adding its examples back into the running totals.

claims.title: Ukraine stage labels cannot be netted casually
claims.summary: Appropriations, drawdowns, disbursements, and delivered-value estimates sit at different stages and remain analytically separate.

claims.title: Military aid keeps net-new lanes separate from already-counted foreign assistance
claims.summary: Section 5 excludes allied-funded sales and already-counted assistance while preserving drawdown value versus replacement-cost separation.

claims.title: ORR is owned as a domestic lane, not an overseas add-on
claims.summary: Refugee and entrant assistance stays in one canonical domestic section so it is not double-counted against foreign-assistance accounts.

claims.title: ORR provider capture remains a first-class unresolved limitation
claims.summary: The public record identifies the ORR lane but does not yet publish a defensible entrant-versus-provider split.

claims.title: Emergency Medicaid is measurable on a federal-plus-state basis, not a federal-only basis
claims.summary: Section 7 preserves the measurable lane while carrying the missing federal-only share as an explicit open question.

claims.title: SNAP noncitizen dollars are not backfilled with modeled estimates
claims.summary: Section 8 documents the eligibility framework and refuses unsupported benefit attribution by population share.

claims.title: SSI is one of the clearest direct-cash domestic lanes
claims.summary: The current edition relies on SSA-published noncitizen recipient and payment inputs while preserving the point-in-time limitation.

claims.title: Housing eligibility rules already exclude many ineligible uses through Section 214 and proration
claims.summary: Section 10 is strongest on program mechanics, not on a missing published eligible-noncitizen outlay total.

claims.title: K-12 and student-aid analysis is constrained by legal collection limits and eligibility structure
claims.summary: Section 11 treats missing education spending ledgers as a real transparency limit rather than something to impute.

claims.title: State-administered federal dollars add zero net-new dollars by design
claims.summary: Section 12 is a routing and reconciliation lens that guards against double counting once federal money passes through state systems.

claims.title: The gap register is itself a finding, not an empty placeholder
claims.summary: Section 13 names programs where the record does not support a defensible citizenship breakout and refuses to fabricate missing values.

claims.title: The conservative total is a reproducible set of subtotals, not one synthetic number
claims.summary: Section 14 is locked to a blueprint that preserves incompatible bases instead of flattening them into a false grand total.

claims.title: Publication limits remain visible and binding
claims.summary: Section 15 preserves missing-record constraints as governance rules for what the audit may claim.

claims.title: The final argument inherits the same basis rules and evidence limits
claims.summary: Section 16 does not create a new theory of the numbers; it restates bounded findings from the locked sections.

decisions.title: Gross, not net
decisions.body: Every adopted figure is gross money directed toward non-U.S. recipients or noncitizens. The audit does not offset those figures against taxes paid or any net-fiscal-impact model.

decisions.title: Status categories are never collapsed
decisions.body: Lawful permanent residents, refugees, asylees, parolees, temporary statuses, and undocumented persons are analytically distinct categories and are never merged into one undifferentiated headline.

decisions.title: Number type and resource category are recorded separately
decisions.body: Each figure keeps both its number type and its resource category visible. Drawdown transfer value, replacement appropriations, obligations, outlays, and program authorities are tracked as different things.

decisions.title: Universe-of-programs completeness artifact
decisions.body: Sections with broad program scope publish their universe explicitly, including residual or immaterial lines, so no material account is silently dropped from the research frame.

decisions.title: The Two Running Totals
decisions.body: The audit preserves two permanent running concepts: Total A for gross directed resources and Total B for estimated value ultimately reaching the non-U.S. recipient after U.S.-captured share. They are never merged.

decisions.title: Section 3 to Section 5 netting rule
decisions.body: Military-aid lines already embedded in the foreign-assistance aggregate are counted once. Section 5 aggregates only NET-NEW military lanes and excludes allied-funded FMS, DCS, and other non-taxpayer or already-counted amounts.

decisions.title: Research docket publication rule
decisions.body: Illustrative sections may publish their search path, source classes, and unresolved pulls so readers can see what was investigated even when those sections are non-additive.

decisions.title: Standardized key-findings opening
decisions.body: Certain locked sections begin with a standardized key-findings block that summarizes already-established evidence without introducing new evidence or new methodology in the summary itself.

decisions.title: No blended grand total
decisions.body: Where lane subtotals sit on incompatible bases such as disbursement versus obligation, annual versus cumulative, or federal versus federal-plus-state, the rigorous output is a set of basis-segregated subtotals rather than one synthetic total.

decisions.title: Ownership map for domestic sections
decisions.body: Domestic federal dollars are owned by one canonical section so routed programs are not counted twice. ORR, Medicaid, TANF-related pathways, and state-administered lenses keep their owning section explicit.

decisions.title: Section 14 blueprint frozen
decisions.body: The conservative-total section is built to a fixed blueprint that preserves lane separation, forbids invented gap estimates, and requires reproducibility from the locked source sections.

decisions.title: Section 14 locked
decisions.body: After the blueprint was populated and checked, Section 14 was locked as the publication's conservative set-of-subtotals representation.

decisions.title: Section 15 locked
decisions.body: The limitations section was locked to carry forward the publication's missing-record constraints without backfilling them with modeled certainty.

decisions.title: Section 16 locked
decisions.body: The final argument was locked after assembly so the publication's closing synthesis reflects the same basis-separation and limitation rules as the rest of Version 1.0.

decisions.title: Repository completion assembly
decisions.body: Repository completion for Version 1.0 means the locked sections, appendices, source library, and decision log are assembled as publication assets without rewriting the underlying analytical conclusions.

openQuestions.title: ForeignAssistance.gov live sum and version date
openQuestions.whyItMatters: Section 3 relies on published aggregate foreign-assistance reporting, but the live-sum and version-stamp execution path still needs a fully pinned primary extraction record.
openQuestions.currentState: Appendix A carries this as the primary-execution item behind the international-assistance aggregate.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A version-stamped primary extraction from ForeignAssistance.gov showing the live sum and retrieval date used for the locked figure.

openQuestions.title: GAO budget-glossary edition
openQuestions.whyItMatters: If a superseding GAO glossary materially changed an adopted accounting definition, the methodology notes would need to carry that custody update explicitly.
openQuestions.currentState: The audit uses GAO-05-734SP and flags the edition-confirmation question instead of silently assuming there is no later definitional change.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: Confirmation that no later GAO budget-glossary edition changes the adopted definitions used in the publication.

openQuestions.title: Post-2025 USAID-State administering offices
openQuestions.whyItMatters: The 2025 realignment creates a continuity problem when later-year international-assistance reporting is compared to prior administrative structures.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 3 carries the post-2025 office realignment as a reporting discontinuity rather than pretending the series is perfectly continuous.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A reconciled post-2025 administrative mapping that ties later reporting offices back to the pre-realignment foreign-assistance series.

openQuestions.title: IMF budget-scoring treatment
openQuestions.whyItMatters: International financial institution support can score differently from direct grant aid, so IMF-related treatment affects scope discipline in the international-assistance universe.
openQuestions.currentState: The publication flags IMF scoring treatment instead of assuming a simple add-or-exclude rule where the budget basis may differ.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A primary budget-scoring record clarifying how the relevant IMF support should be treated on the audit's chosen basis.

openQuestions.title: Unpublished U.S.-capture share in international assistance
openQuestions.whyItMatters: Without a published capture-share breakout, the site can report gross taxpayer assistance (Total A) but cannot compute a defensible Total B for international assistance.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 3 states that Total B is materially below Total A, but not measurable from the current public record.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: Program-level reporting that distinguishes value captured by U.S. entities from value reaching non-U.S. recipients.

openQuestions.title: Ukraine stage figures from the primary portal
openQuestions.whyItMatters: Ukraine assistance is often quoted across incompatible stages, so a primary stage-by-stage portal view is necessary to keep appropriation, obligation, disbursement, and delivery claims distinct.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 4 confirms the structural stage gap but still notes that dynamic portal extraction was not fully captured in the locked artifact path.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A stable primary-portal extract that shows Ukraine aid by stage using consistent definitions.

openQuestions.title: PDA valuation treatment
openQuestions.whyItMatters: Presidential Drawdown Authority values can diverge from replacement cost, and valuation ambiguity can create false inflation if the wrong measure is reused elsewhere.
openQuestions.currentState: The publication treats drawdown value and replacement appropriation separately and carries the valuation problem as a reporting-quality issue.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: Primary valuation documentation clarifying the exact basis used for PDA transfer values in the compared reporting streams.

openQuestions.title: Ukraine grant-versus-loan treatment
openQuestions.whyItMatters: Some Ukraine support is repayable or collateralized, so grant-versus-loan treatment changes what can be treated as direct transferred value.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 4 flags the issue and avoids flattening repayable or collateralized support into simple grant totals.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A primary classification record separating grant-value support from loans, guarantees, or other repayable structures.

openQuestions.title: Israel FMF/MOU staging details
openQuestions.whyItMatters: Israel aid lines are cleaner than Ukraine's, but stage discipline still matters when distinguishing base MOU commitments from supplemental appropriations and execution status.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 4 confirms the main Israel statutory lines while carrying narrower staging details as a residual verification item.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A fully staged primary record that ties MOU, supplemental, and execution-state views into one reference chain.

openQuestions.title: Capture share for Ukraine and Israel military aid
openQuestions.whyItMatters: Gross military-aid figures do not equal value ultimately reaching the foreign recipient because replenishment, U.S. manufacturing, and domestic capture can absorb a large share.
openQuestions.currentState: The publication keeps Total B open for these military cases rather than inventing a capture rate.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: Program-level evidence showing what share of military-aid appropriations and obligations is captured in the United States versus reaching the foreign recipient.

openQuestions.title: Israel OSP share
openQuestions.whyItMatters: The offshore-procurement share affects how much FMF can be spent in Israel rather than on U.S. defense articles.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 4 uses the published OSP framing but flags the exact current share as an open verification item.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A current primary record for the exact OSP share and its implementation window.

openQuestions.title: Israel 2024 supplemental obligated-versus-outlaid status
openQuestions.whyItMatters: Appropriation alone does not show execution stage, and the 2024 supplemental should not be narrated as outlaid if only obligated or appropriated.
openQuestions.currentState: The publication keeps the stage distinction explicit and records the unresolved execution-state detail as an open item.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: Primary execution reporting showing whether the relevant supplemental amount is appropriated, obligated, or outlaid.

openQuestions.title: DoD execution-level annual figures for Section 333, CTEF, and ASFF
openQuestions.whyItMatters: The recurring military lane is built from annual programs, so execution-level figures matter for staying on the right basis and year.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 5 carries approximate recurring figures while noting that exact current-year execution detail is still incomplete.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: Primary DoD execution-level annual figures for Section 333, CTEF, and related annual security-cooperation programs.

openQuestions.title: EDA marginal taxpayer cost versus acquisition value
openQuestions.whyItMatters: Excess Defense Articles can be described by acquisition value or marginal taxpayer cost, and the distinction affects whether any figure belongs in a transfer total.
openQuestions.currentState: The publication leaves EDA as a marginal or pending item instead of forcing a misleading value into the annual military lane.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A primary cost treatment record showing the marginal taxpayer cost basis for EDA transfers.

openQuestions.title: WRSA transferred value versus retained U.S.-owned stock
openQuestions.whyItMatters: War Reserve Stockpiles for Allies can blur the line between transferred value and still-U.S.-owned stock, which affects whether a taxpayer transfer has actually occurred.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 5 leaves WRSA out of the adopted subtotal until a defensible transfer-value basis is available.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A primary record separating transferred WRSA value from stock that remains U.S.-owned or merely positioned.

openQuestions.title: SDAF revolving treatment
openQuestions.whyItMatters: The Special Defense Acquisition Fund could look like an added military-aid pool if its revolving nature is ignored.
openQuestions.currentState: Appendix A marks this resolved: SDAF is revolving, nets to approximately $0 in the audit frame, and is excluded from adopted totals.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: No further record needed for v1.0; carried as resolved in the register.

openQuestions.title: Cross-section harmonization between foreign-assistance and military-aid lanes
openQuestions.whyItMatters: Section 3 uses single-year obligations while Section 5 uses cumulative multi-year military lanes, so a blended headline would overstate precision.
openQuestions.currentState: Appendix A marks this resolved into lanes: the platform preserves basis-segregated subtotals instead of forcing a synthetic grand total.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A lawful harmonization method that keeps measure type, period, and duplication risks separated.

openQuestions.title: ORR consolidated outlay and recipient/provider split
openQuestions.whyItMatters: The refugee-resettlement lane is routed through states, voluntary agencies, and providers, but the public record does not cleanly isolate value reaching entrants.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 6 names the program and the gap, but leaves Total B unresolved.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: Public ORR outlay reporting that isolates entrant-directed value versus provider or operator capture.

openQuestions.title: ORR entrant-versus-provider split
openQuestions.whyItMatters: A large share of ORR funds flows to states, voluntary agencies, and shelter operators rather than directly as cash to entrants.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 6 states that the entrant-versus-provider split is unpublished and therefore leaves Total B materially below but not measurable from the public record.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: Program-level public reporting that separates entrant-directed value from provider, operator, and administrative capture.

openQuestions.title: UC in-scope versus custodial share
openQuestions.whyItMatters: The Unaccompanied Children program dominates ORR spending, but its custodial and support components are not cleanly partitioned for this audit's beneficiary logic.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 6 carries UC's dominance openly while leaving the in-scope beneficiary split unresolved.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A public breakout distinguishing the UC share that should count as entrant-directed support from custodial or operator-heavy expenditures.

openQuestions.title: Afghan and Ukrainian parolee supplemental amounts
openQuestions.whyItMatters: Supplemental entrant populations can change ORR composition materially, but the public record does not fully isolate the amounts attributable to those parolee cohorts.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 6 keeps the parolee supplements visible as a data-gap item rather than allocating them heuristically.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: Public ORR reporting that isolates Afghan and Ukrainian parolee supplemental amounts on the same basis as the main ORR lane.

openQuestions.title: CMS-64 emergency Medicaid federal share by year
openQuestions.whyItMatters: The publication has a published emergency Medicaid total, but the federal-only share by year is needed for a cleaner federal total.
openQuestions.currentState: The item is carried in the register with MACPAC and CBO corroboration but without the federal-only annual breakout.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A year-specific CMS-64-based federal-share breakout for emergency Medicaid.

openQuestions.title: Eligible-immigrant Medicaid outlay
openQuestions.whyItMatters: Eligible-immigrant Medicaid spending may be material, but the public record does not publish a clean status-based outlay line for that population.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 7 routes this gap to Section 13 rather than fabricating a status-based Medicaid figure.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A published eligible-immigrant Medicaid outlay series on a federal basis.

openQuestions.title: Reconcile House Budget and CBO figures versus KFF $3.8B
openQuestions.whyItMatters: Large emergency Medicaid figures from different sources can look contradictory unless their time windows are made explicit.
openQuestions.currentState: Appendix A marks this reconciled: the figures rest on the same dataset family but different time windows, as shown in Table 14-H.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: No additional v1.0 record is needed beyond the time-window explanation already carried in the publication.

openQuestions.title: SNAP noncitizen participant share and benefit attribution
openQuestions.whyItMatters: Participant composition is published, but a benefit-dollar breakout net of citizen children is not, and multiplying the participation share by the total would be prohibited modeling.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 8 publishes the participation mix and explicitly routes the missing dollar attribution to Section 13.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A published SNAP spending breakout isolating noncitizen-attributable benefits net of citizen-child portions.

openQuestions.title: WIC and school-meals status-neutrality
openQuestions.whyItMatters: Programs without status conditions may be legally or operationally unable to generate a noncitizen-specific spending line.
openQuestions.currentState: The publication treats these programs as unresolved Section 13 gaps rather than inferring a noncitizen share.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A lawful published record showing whether any status-based breakout exists for these status-neutral nutrition programs.

openQuestions.title: TANF eligible-noncitizen share net of ORR
openQuestions.whyItMatters: TANF is heavily citizen-child-dominated and can overlap with ORR-routed assistance, so the noncitizen share cannot be cleanly derived from published totals.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 9 fences ORR-routed support back to Section 6 and sends the unresolved TANF share question to Section 13.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A published TANF breakout for eligible noncitizen benefits net of ORR-routed assistance.

openQuestions.title: SSI point-in-time basis versus annual flow basis
openQuestions.status: Open (figure carried at Medium, Dec-2021 basis)
openQuestions.whyItMatters: The SSI figure is computed from a published December 2021 basis, which is strong for that point in time but not a full annual transactional ledger.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 9 keeps the figure labeled on its Dec. 2021 basis and avoids overstating it as a harmonized annual program total.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A published annual SSI spending breakout for noncitizen recipients or a fully reconciled monthly series.

openQuestions.title: ACTC/CTC via ITIN and citizen-child share
openQuestions.whyItMatters: Tax-credit flows can involve ITIN filers and citizen children, which makes noncitizen-specific attribution impossible without a published IRS breakout.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 9 names the gap and routes it to Section 13 instead of deriving a figure from mixed-status tax filing patterns.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: An IRS-published breakout isolating ACTC or CTC amounts attributable to the noncitizen population without citizen-child overlap.

openQuestions.title: HUD eligible-noncitizen prorated outlay
openQuestions.whyItMatters: Housing assistance is already screened for ineligible members, but a clean eligible-noncitizen spending line is still not published.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 10 documents the structural rules and closest figure, then routes the missing noncitizen-specific outlay to Section 13.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A HUD-published outlay breakout for eligible noncitizen housing assistance after proration and citizen-member separation.

openQuestions.title: No-verification grant housing programs
openQuestions.whyItMatters: Grant-funded housing programs that do not verify immigration status cannot easily produce a status-based spending breakout.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 10 marks these programs as unresolved rather than assigning them a modeled share.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A published program-level record showing whether any status breakout exists inside no-verification housing grants.

openQuestions.title: Federal K-12 noncitizen spending
openQuestions.whyItMatters: K-12 schooling is constitutionally status-blind, so the public record may not be able to produce a status-based federal spending line at all.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 11 treats this as a legal collection limit rather than a simple missing dataset.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: No ordinary status-based spending dataset is expected; any lawful resolution would need to respect Plyler's status-collection limits.

openQuestions.title: Eligible-noncitizen federal student aid
openQuestions.whyItMatters: Undocumented students are barred from federal aid, but eligible-noncitizen aid is still not published as a separate spending line.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 11 names the eligibility boundary clearly and routes the missing eligible-noncitizen spending breakout to Section 13.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A Department of Education breakout isolating federal student-aid amounts for eligible noncitizens.

openQuestions.title: CCDF, SSBG, CSBG, and LIHEAP no-breakout
openQuestions.whyItMatters: State-administered federal streams can lose citizenship detail in the federal-state-local chain, preventing a clean noncitizen spending breakout.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 12 documents the reconciliation role and routes these no-breakout programs into the Section 13 gap register.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: Published program breakouts showing whether any of these state-administered federal streams isolate status-level spending.

openQuestions.title: Per-program responsible agency for missing status data
openQuestions.whyItMatters: Readers need to know which agency would have to publish a missing status-based breakout before a gap can ever be resolved.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 13 frames this as part of the gap register rather than pretending every missing line has the same administrative owner.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A program-by-program ownership map tying each missing breakout to the responsible federal reporting agency.

openQuestions.title: Whether Class C or D modeled estimates exist as bounded context only
openQuestions.whyItMatters: Modeled or secondary estimates may exist for some gaps, but using them as adopted totals would violate the audit's evidence hierarchy unless they are clearly bounded as context only.
openQuestions.currentState: Section 13 leaves this open instead of backfilling opaque programs with secondary modeled estimates.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A bounded inventory of Class C or D estimates that can be published as context without replacing missing primary evidence.

openQuestions.title: Federal-only emergency Medicaid share
openQuestions.whyItMatters: The site can publish the federal-plus-state emergency Medicaid figure used in the locked section, but not the federal-only share needed for a cleaner federal total.
openQuestions.currentState: The limitation is carried forward explicitly in the Executive Summary and Section 7.
openQuestions.recordNeeded: A public federal-only emergency Medicaid spending breakout for the same period and population basis.
